blocks.body: The square root of n (written sqrt(n)) is the number that, when squared, equals n. sqrt(25) = 5 because 5^2 = 25. Every positive number has two square roots: positive and negative.
A number whose square root is a whole number. Examples: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100.
The cube root of n (written as the cube root symbol or n^(1/3)) is the number that, when cubed, equals n. Cube root of 27 = 3 because 3^3 = 27. Cube root of -8 = -2 because (-2)^3 = -8.
Square roots of negative numbers are not real numbers. sqrt(-4) is not real. However, cube roots of negative numbers are real: cube root of -27 = -3.

blocks.label: Examples
blocks.body: sqrt(64) = 8. sqrt(144) = 12. cube root of 8 = 2. cube root of 125 = 5. sqrt(0.25) = 0.5. sqrt(1/9) = 1/3.
On a calculator, sqrt(x) finds the principal (positive) square root. To find the negative root, add a minus sign: -sqrt(x).
